attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old non-binary person presents to the General Practice clinic at 36 weeks of pregnancy for routine antenatal care. They have no significant medical history and do not take any regular medications. They report using a chest binder regularly to manage gender dysphoria but express concern about continuing this practice after birth. They plan to chestfeed their baby but are anxious about the physical changes associated with lactation.

On examination, they appear well with vital signs within normal limits. Examination of the chest reveals no abnormalities, with normal nipple appearance and no tenderness.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate advice regarding chest binding during the postpartum period?
options: Chest binding can be safely continued if the binder is removed for feeding sessions
Chest binding for up to 12 hours daily is safe during lactation
Chest binding should be avoided during lactation due to increased mastitis risk
Chest binding should be avoided during the early postpartum period (first 6 - 12 weeks or until milk supply is established)
Chest binding is safe during lactation provided the patient feed or expresses every 2-3 hours to maintain milk flow
attributes.explanation:
This case involves a non-binary person who uses chest binding to manage gender dysphoria and plans to chestfeed after birth. Understanding the relationship between chest binding and lactation complications is crucial for providing appropriate, gender-affirming care.

The mastitis spectrum, as described in current lactation guidelines, progresses from ductal narrowing and inflammatory mastitis to bacterial mastitis. External compression from binding can exacerbate these processes by impeding milk flow and causing milk stasis, particularly during the high-risk early postpartum period when secretory activation and engorgement occur.

Chest binding should be avoided during the early postpartum period (first 6 - 12 weeks or until milk supply is established) is correct. Trans-affirming harm-reduction guidance specifically advises avoiding binding in the first two to three months postpartum because binding likely reduces milk production and increases risk of inflammation and mastitis. After supply is established (typically by 2-3 months), occasional binding may be cautiously reintroduced with modifications to decrease feelings of dysphoria and prolong the bodyfeeding relationship. Importantly, for patients who chose the chestfeed and reintroduce binding, education about monitoring for signs of mastitis (e.g. erythema, pain, warmth, swelling in the breast) and reducing milk supply (e.g. changes in infant feeding behaviour such as fussiness after feeds or increasing frequency of feeds) is important as binding at any stage in the chestfeeding journey may increase the risk of mastitis and/or reducing milk supply.

Chest binding can be safely continued if the binder is removed for feeding sessions is incorrect. Removing the binder only for feeds doesn't eliminate between-feed compression during the high-risk early postpartum period. The effects of compression don't immediately resolve when the binder is removed, and repeated compression can cause cumulative damage to breast tissue and milk ducts.

Chest binding for up to 12 hours daily is safe during lactation is incorrect. Even general (non-lactation) binder safety guidelines advise a maximum of 8-10 hours with breaks and no overnight binding. There is less tolerance during lactation, especially in the early postpartum period, making 12 hours unsafe and likely to increase complications.

Chest binding should be avoided during lactation due to increased mastitis risk is overly rigid and not aligned with harm-reduction principles. After milk supply is established, many guidelines allow cautious, adjusted binding to support gender euphoria while mitigating risk. This approach respects both lactation physiology and gender affirmation needs.

Chest binding is safe during lactation provided the patient feed or expresses every 2-3 hours to maintain milk flow is incorrect. While frequent milk removal is essential for preventing mastitis, it does not negate the mechanical compression effects of binding. The physical pressure from binding can cause ductal narrowing and impede milk flow regardless of feeding frequency. Additionally, binding can make it more difficult to fully empty the breast during feeds or expression, increasing the risk of milk stasis even with frequent removal attempts.

When reintroducing binding after supply is established, harm-reduction strategies include: sizing up the binder compared to pre-pregnancy size, limiting duration to 8 hours or less with breaks, avoiding overnight binding, removing for all feeds, considering less-compressive alternatives on high-supply days, and monitoring closely for signs of mastitis or ductal narrowing. Healthcare providers should work collaboratively with patients to create individualised plans that align lactation goals with gender-affirming needs, potentially involving lactation consultants experienced in trans and non-binary care.
learningPoints: Chest binding during early lactation (first 6 - 12 weeks) significantly increases mastitis risk through milk stasis and ductal compression.
After milk supply is established, binding may be cautiously reintroduced with modifications including sizing up, limiting duration to 8 hours, and removing for feeds.
Gender-affirming lactation care requires individualised planning that balances mastitis prevention with psychological wellbeing.
references: Trans Care BC. Binding during pregnancy & bodyfeeding. 2024. Available at: https://www.transcarebc.ca/sites/default/files/2024-03/Binding%20during%20pregnancy%20%26%20bodyfeeding.pdf
NHS. Chestfeeding if you're trans or non-binary. Available at: https://www.nhs.uk/pregnancy/having-a-baby-if-you-are-lgbt-plus/chestfeeding-if-youre-trans-or-non-binary/
Oregon Health & Science University. Chest Binding Safety. 2022. Available at: https://www.ohsu.edu/sites/default/files/2022-03/Gender-Clinic-Chest-Binding-Handout.pdf
Australian Breastfeeding Association. Mastitis. Available at: https://www.breastfeeding.asn.au/resources/mastitis

Mitchell KB, Johnson HM, Rodríguez JM, et al. Academy of Breastfeeding Medicine Clinical Protocol #36: The Mastitis Spectrum, Revised 2022. Breastfeed Med. 2022;17(5):360-376. Available at: https://www.bfmed.org/assets/ABM%20Protocol%20%2336.pdf
